chunk_summary: Guidance on anthropometry and growth interpretation in children with neurodisability, emphasizing alternative measures like segmental lengths and MUAC, and cautious use of growth charts for nutritional assessment.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of traditional weight and height measurements in children with neurodisability.
Learn alternative anthropometric methods such as segmental measures and MUAC for growth assessment.
Interpret growth charts and body composition proxies in the context of neurodisability.
Recognize the importance of tracking trends over time rather than relying on single measurements.
key_facts: Weight and height alone are insufficient for nutritional assessment in children with neurodisability due to factors like scoliosis and altered body composition.
Segmental measures such as knee height, tibial length, or ulna length can estimate stature when standing height is unreliable.
Mid-upper arm circumference (MUAC) and triceps skinfold are useful proxies for body composition but should be interpreted cautiously in severe CP.
WHO growth charts are appropriate up to 5 years of age if reliable height data is available; CP-specific charts can contextualize growth but should not normalize undernutrition.
Persistent low weight or falling growth velocity should trigger active nutritional intervention, regardless of the growth chart used.
reference_quote: "Use segmental measures to estimate stature. Knee height or tibial length can be measured with a caliper or measuring tape and converted to estimated height using validated equations." (Source: ESPGHAN Advice Guide)
document_key_topics: Alternative anthropometric methods for children with neurodisability
Use of growth charts and body composition proxies
Nutritional risk assessment in severe CP
